what: Why a simple ratio map is not enough
title: Childcare accessibility with a gravity allocation model
text: simple ratio map compares number childcare places number children spatial unit. easy read, assumes demand served locally. strong limitation, especially dense urban areas families may use childcare outside sector.
code: data(ratio_map)  communes_bxl <- phacochr::phaco_data("communes_bxl")  mf_map(ratio_map, col = NA, border = "grey") mf_map(communes_bxl, col = NA, border = "black", add = TRUE)  mf_map(   ratio_map,   var = c("supply_sector", "ratio"),   type = "prop_choro",   pal = "Viridis",   nbreaks = 5,   inches = 0.08,   leg_title = c("Childcare places", "Places per 100 children"),   leg_pos = c("topright", "topleft"),   border = "black",   lwd = 0.3,   add = TRUE )  mf_layout(   title = "Local ratio of childcare places to children",   credits = "IGEAT-ULB, 2026.",   arrow = FALSE,   frame = TRUE )

title: Childcare accessibility with a gravity allocation model
text: gravity model allocates demand supply locations according supply capacity, demand levels, travel distance. Initial interactions estimated using gravity function: Iij=Si*Djdij2 I_{ij} = \frac{S_i * D_j}{d_{ij}^{2}} SiS_i supply, DjD_j demand, dijd_{ij} distance demand supply locations. Furness algorithm iteratively adjusts interactions total allocated demand matches available supply satisfying demand constraints. resulting allocation reflects competition limited resources travel distance, producing accessibility measure accounts service saturation spatial separation users facilities.
code: distance_matrix <- distance_matrix(   supply = supply,   demand = demand,   min_distance = 400 )  allocation <- gravity(   supply = supply,   demand = demand,   distance_matrix = distance_matrix,   distance_power = 2,   max_iter = 100,   delta = 0.001,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Childcare accessibility with a gravity allocation model
text: resulting allocation can used calculate average travel distance demand location. case population-based analyses, indicator can mapped statistical sector. High average distances generally indicate local shortage supply relative demand. Residents areas must travel access services nearby facilities insufficient already saturated. Mapping average distances therefore provides simple way identify spatial inequalities accessibility areas additional capacity may needed.
code: sec_bxl <- phacochr::phaco_data("sec_bxl")  demand_map <- sec_bxl |>   left_join(allocation$demand, by = c("cd_sector2024" = "id"))  supply_sf <- allocation$supply |>   st_as_sf(coords = c("x", "y"), crs = 31370)  mf_map(demand_map, col = NA, border = "grey") mf_map(communes_bxl, col = NA, border = "black", add = TRUE)  mf_map(   demand_map,   var = c("demand", "mean_distance"),   type = "prop_choro",   pal = "Viridis",   nbreaks = 6,   inches = 0.09,   leg_title = c("Children", "Mean distance"),   leg_pos = c("topright", "topleft"),   border = "black",   lwd = 0.3,   add = TRUE )  mf_map(   supply_sf,   type = "prop",   var = "supply",   inches = 0.04,   col = "red",   leg_title = "Childcare capacity",   leg_pos = "left",   border = NA,   add = TRUE )  mf_layout(   title = "Mean distance from demand locations",   credits = "IGEAT-ULB, 2026.",   arrow = FALSE,   frame = TRUE )

title: Authors and Citation
text: Hugo Perilleux. Author, maintainer. Pierre Marissal. Author.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Perilleux H, Marissal P (2026). gravitr: Gravity-Based Allocation Models. R package version 0.9, https://ulb-igeat.github.io/gravitr/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {gravitr: Gravity-Based Allocation Models},   author = {Hugo Perilleux and Pierre Marissal},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.9},   url = {https://ulb-igeat.github.io/gravitr/}, }

title: Gravity-Based Allocation Models
text: gravitR implements gravity-based allocation models using Furness convergence algorithm estimate interactions demand locations service providers. package particularly suited study accessibility public services collective amenities, including childcare, education, healthcare, sports facilities. combining distance-decay effects capacity constraints competition users, gravitR produces accessibility indicators can used measure spatial inequalities access services facilities. indicators reflect proximity service saturation, providing realistic assessment territorial equity supporting evidence-based planning policy making.

title: Gravity-Based Allocation Models
code: # Install devtools if necessary install.packages("devtools")  # Install gravitr devtools::install_github("ULB-IGEAT/gravitr") library(gravitr) library(dplyr) library(sf) library(mapsf) library(phacochr) library(gravitr)

what: Why a simple ratio map is not enough
title: Gravity-Based Allocation Models
text: simple ratio map compares number childcare places number children spatial unit. easy read, assumes demand served locally. strong limitation, especially dense urban areas families may use childcare outside sector. addition, ratio calculated areas resident population, even though childcare facilities may located serve neighbouring areas.
code: data(ratio_map)  communes_bxl <- phacochr::phaco_data("communes_bxl")  mf_map(ratio_map, col = NA, border = "grey") mf_map(communes_bxl, col = NA, border = "black", add = TRUE)  mf_map(   ratio_map,   var = c("supply_sector", "ratio"),   type = "prop_choro",   pal = "Viridis",   nbreaks = 5,   inches = 0.08,   leg_title = c("Childcare places", "Places per 100 children"),   leg_pos = c("topright", "topleft"),   border = "black",   lwd = 0.3,   add = TRUE )  mf_layout(   title = "Local ratio of childcare places to children",   credits = "IGEAT-ULB, 2026.",   arrow = FALSE,   frame = TRUE )

title: Gravity-Based Allocation Models
text: gravity model allocates demand supply locations according supply capacity, demand levels, travel distance. Initial interactions estimated using gravity function: Iij=Si*Djdij2I_{ij} = \frac{S_i * D_j}{d_{ij}^{2}} SiS_i supply, DjD_j demand, dijd_{ij} distance demand supply locations. Furness algorithm iteratively adjusts interactions total allocated demand matches available supply satisfying demand constraints. resulting allocation reflects competition limited resources travel distance, producing accessibility measure accounts service saturation spatial separation users facilities.
code: distance_matrix <- distance_matrix(   supply = supply,   demand = demand,   min_distance = 400 )  allocation <- gravity(   supply = supply,   demand = demand,   distance_matrix = distance_matrix,   distance_power = 2,   max_iter = 100,   delta = 0.001,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Gravity-Based Allocation Models
text: resulting allocation can used calculate average travel distance demand location. case population-based analyses, indicator can mapped statistical sector. High average distances generally indicate local shortage supply relative demand. Residents areas must travel access services nearby facilities insufficient already saturated. Mapping average distances therefore provides simple way identify spatial inequalities accessibility areas additional capacity may needed.
code: sec_bxl <- phacochr::phaco_data("sec_bxl")  demand_map <- sec_bxl |>   left_join(allocation$demand, by = c("cd_sector2024" = "id"))  supply_sf <- allocation$supply |>   st_as_sf(coords = c("x", "y"), crs = 31370)  mf_map(demand_map, col = NA, border = "grey") mf_map(communes_bxl, col = NA, border = "black", add = TRUE)  mf_map(   demand_map,   var = c("demand", "mean_distance"),   type = "prop_choro",   pal = "Viridis",   nbreaks = 6,   inches = 0.09,   leg_title = c("Children", "Mean distance"),   leg_pos = c("topright", "topleft"),   border = "black",   lwd = 0.3,   add = TRUE )  mf_map(   supply_sf,   type = "prop",   var = "supply",   inches = 0.04,   col = "red",   leg_title = "Childcare capacity",   leg_pos = "left",   border = NA,   add = TRUE )  mf_layout(   title = "Mean distance from demand locations",   credits = "IGEAT-ULB, 2026.",   arrow = FALSE,   frame = TRUE )

title: Compute a distance matrix — compute_distance_matrix
text: supply data frame containing supply locations. demand data frame containing demand locations. supply_x Name x-coordinate column supply. supply_y Name y-coordinate column supply. demand_x Name x-coordinate column demand. demand_y Name y-coordinate column demand. min_distance Minimum distance value. Distances value replaced.

title: Compute a distance matrix — compute_distance_matrix
text: numeric matrix supply units rows demand units columns.

text: supply data frame containing supply locations. demand data frame containing demand locations. supply_x Name x-coordinate column supply. supply_y Name y-coordinate column supply. demand_x Name x-coordinate column demand. demand_y Name y-coordinate column demand. min_distance Minimum distance value. what: Allocate supply and demand using a gravity model with Furness convergence — gravity
title: Allocate supply and demand using a gravity model with Furness convergence — gravity
text: Allocate supply demand using gravity model Furness convergence

title: Allocate supply and demand using a gravity model with Furness convergence — gravity
code: gravity(   supply,   demand,   distance_matrix,   supply_col = "supply",   demand_col = "demand",   distance_power = 2,   max_iter = 100,   delta = 0.001,   min_improvement = 1e-04,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Allocate supply and demand using a gravity model with Furness convergence — gravity
text: supply data frame containing supply values. demand data frame containing demand values. distance_matrix matrix distances. Rows must match supply, columns must match demand. supply_col Name supply column. demand_col Name demand column. distance_power Power applied distance initial gravity formula. max_iter Maximum number iterations. delta Convergence threshold. min_improvement Minimum relative improvement stopping. verbose TRUE, prints convergence messages.

title: Allocate supply and demand using a gravity model with Furness convergence — gravity
text: list containing flows, weighted distances, summaries convergence information.
